pattern: A pairwise 'product is a perfect square' condition on indices collapses into a plain grouping problem once you strip every squared factor out of each index: two indices are allowed together exactly when what's left over afterward matches.
hints: The compatibility of two indices only depends on the indices themselves, never on the values in nums -- so first work out, using only 1..n, which indices are even allowed to sit in the same subset.

i * j is a perfect square exactly when i and j reduce to the same value after you repeatedly divide out any factor that appears squared -- try peeling squared factors off an index and see what's left over.
Bucket every index from 1 to n by that leftover square-free remainder, add up nums within each bucket, and the answer is just the largest bucket total.

edge_inputs.description: a single index has no partner to conflict with
two indices whose product is not a perfect square, so they must land in separate buckets
index 1 and index 4 are compatible (product 4), but the biggest lone index can still beat their combined bucket
a large value sits on a non-square-adjacent index and must not get folded into the 1/4/9 bucket
indices 1, 4 and 9 are mutually compatible and one of them carries most of the weight
extreme low and extreme high values on two incompatible indices
every value identical, so the answer is decided purely by which bucket has the most indices
a moderately large index range to exercise several square-free buckets at once

stress.description: random values across a large index range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 10000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 10**9) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
